text: Hello friends this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy and cholecystectomy in the same patient and we are planning to perform this surgery by three ports only where you will remove the myoma as well as gall bladder with the three ports. Myoma is quite large it is approximately twelve centimeter of the fibroid and patient has acute cholecystitis as well. so let us see we will go with the Palmer's point that is the mid-clavicular line just below the costal margin and this will be our entry and after that we will put the one port epigastric that we will use for telescoping and that we will use for cholecystectomy also And one port we will use in lumber area. And we will keep on transferring the telescope to epigastric and to the hypochondrium. So this is right hypochondrium where you will use right hand. This is left hypochondrium. And right now epigastric latter we will use for camera. So access we will do by the varus needle. This is varus needle is introduced in the Palmer's point.

The surgeon performs irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm proper placement of the varus needle. Pneumoperitoneum is initiated, visibly distending the abdomen. The varus needle is removed, and the incision is enlarged to 11 mm for the introduction of a 10 mm optical trocar at Palmer's point.

The surgeon examines the abdomen using the epigastric port, positioned just left of the falciform ligament. The telescope is introduced through this port for diagnostic purposes and will be used for both cholecystectomy and myomectomy. Adhesions are visible on the anterior abdominal wall around the umbilicus, which are being carefully removed.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, facilitating clear access for the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon separates the omentum to proceed with the myomectomy. An aspiration needle is used to inject vasopressin, creating a marble white appearance in the tissue due to hydro dissection and transient ischemia. A myoma screw is then used to hold the large intramural myoma, and an elliptical incision is made on the thickened anterior uterine wall to access and separate the myoma from the uterus.

The surgeon uses a solid, blunt myoma rod to slowly separate the myoma from the uterine wall. The myoma screw is relocated to the base for better grip, as the soft myoma with central fluid degeneration poses a challenge for secure holding. The procedure is performed using three ports, with ambidextrous handling to facilitate the enucleation process.

The surgeon carefully dissects the base of the myoma using harmonic energy to avoid opening the cavity, as the diameter of the vessels is larger at the base. The myoma screw is removed and repositioned to maximize cranial traction, facilitated by the telescope in the epigastric port. The myoma is successfully extracted, and the surgeon begins suturing, introducing the suture through the serosa and performing continuous suturing of the deep muscle layer.

The surgeon begins continuous suturing of the serous layer of the uterus, following completion of the inner muscle layer suturing. Using the left hand for traction and the right hand for suturing, the edges are carefully inverted to ensure proper repair without external protrusion. The procedure is performed using three ports without additional stabilization tools.

The surgeon uses a strong number one Y grill suture to take individual bites, pulling each stitch to tighten the closure. This technique is employed due to the distance between the edges, ensuring a secure and tight repair.
The surgeon adjusts the suturing technique by taking additional bites to ensure the suture is tight and secure, occasionally stepping back to reinforce areas that appear loose.

The surgeon tightens the inner layer of the sutured area using a surgeon's knot, then removes the needle by cutting the suture near it. A small sub-serosal myoma is removed using harmonic dissection and placed over the anterior cul-de-sac for safekeeping. The procedure transitions to a cholecystectomy using a 45 cm instrument from the left lumbar region for traction, with the telescope positioned at Palmer's point. The surgery is performed through three ports, including an epigastric port, despite the challenges posed by the left-sided instrument approach.

The light cable is positioned at nine o'clock to improve visibility during adhesiolysis. The surgeon strips adhesions from the Hartsman pouch and cystic pedicle. The distended stomach is visible due to the telescope's position at the palmar point. The posterior peritoneum is separated, revealing the lymph node of Lund.

The surgeon creates an anterior window between the cystic duct and artery, completing the dissection of the cystic pedicle. A titanium clip is applied from the epigastric port, with the first clip placed towards the common bile duct, the second towards the gallbladder, and a third clip positioned 3 millimeters above the first. The cystic duct is then cut with scissors, and the cystic artery is clipped.

A titanium clip is applied over the cystic artery, followed by coagulation using a harmonic device. The surgeon begins separating the gallbladder from the liver by applying anteromedial and anterolateral traction. The peritoneum is opened first, and the gallbladder is pulled towards the appendix using a single instrument for traction, aiming for a bloodless separation by achieving a good areolar plane.

The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder, marking the end of the cholecystectomy. The gallbladder is extracted through the epigastric port. The process of morcellation begins, which involves careful and slow tissue fragmentation. The morcellator used is an Indian model, as Johnson and Johnson's version has been withdrawn. The procedure concludes with port closure.
